authorship_evidence: Project created as an OWASP workshop teaching tool by Riccardo ten Cate, consistent human coding style throughout, pre-LLM era patterns

evidence.description: SQL injection via string concatenation: db.execute('SELECT pageId, title, content FROM pages WHERE pageId='+pageId). The pageId parameter comes directly from the URL path /dashboard/<pageId> without any sanitization or parameterization.

Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) via the 404 error handler. The request URL is inserted into a template string using Python .format() on line 94, then passed to render_template_string() on line 95. An attacker can inject Jinja2 template expressions (e.g., {{7*7}}) into the URL, achieving RCE. Combined with the system_call context processor from Monitoring.py, this enables direct OS command execution via {{system_call('id')}}.

Insecure pickle deserialization. User-supplied hex-encoded data from request.form['data_obj'] is decoded, written to a file, then loaded with pickle.load() on line 27. Pickle deserialization of untrusted data allows arbitrary code execution via crafted __reduce__ methods.

Insecure YAML deserialization. User-supplied base64-encoded input from the URL path is decoded and passed to yaml.load() without specifying a safe Loader (line 18). With pyyaml 3.13 (from requirements.txt), yaml.load() uses the full Loader by default, allowing arbitrary Python object instantiation and code execution via !!python/object tags.

Reflected XSS via disabled autoescaping. The search parameter from user input is rendered in the template with {% autoescape false %}{{search}}{% endautoescape %} on line 13. The controller (Search.py:9-10) passes the raw request.form.get('search') value directly to the template. Additionally, X-XSS-Protection is explicitly set to '0' in the controller.

XML External Entity (XXE) injection. User-supplied XML from request.form['customers'] is parsed by xml.dom.pulldom.parseString() on line 15 without disabling external entity resolution. This allows an attacker to read local files, perform SSRF, or cause DoS via entity expansion.

Path traversal / arbitrary file read. The fileName parameter from request.form['example_file'] (line 29) is used directly in open() (line 30) and send_file() (line 31) without any path validation. An attacker can supply paths like '../../../../etc/passwd' to read arbitrary files from the server.

Path traversal in file upload. The uploaded filename (file.filename) is used directly in file.save('uploads/'+filename) on line 48 without sanitization via werkzeug.utils.secure_filename(). An attacker can craft a filename containing '../' sequences to write files to arbitrary locations on the server. Although extension checking exists via allowed_file(), the filename itself is not sanitized for directory traversal.

OS command execution via os.popen(). The system_call function on line 13 executes arbitrary OS commands via os.popen(command).read(). This function is registered as a Jinja2 context processor available to ALL templates. While the monitoring template uses hardcoded commands, the SSTI vulnerability in the 404 handler allows an attacker to invoke {{system_call('arbitrary_command')}} for full RCE.

Hardcoded admin credentials. The login function checks password == 'admin' and username == 'admin' on line 12, exposing plaintext credentials in source code. Anyone with access to the source can authenticate as admin.

Hardcoded weak Flask SECRET_KEY set to 'random' on line 5. Flask uses SECRET_KEY to cryptographically sign session cookies. A known/weak secret key allows an attacker to forge session cookies, bypass authentication, and impersonate any user.

Flask debug mode enabled in production (app.debug = True on line 6). This exposes the Werkzeug interactive debugger, which allows arbitrary Python code execution in the browser. It also exposes detailed stack traces, environment variables, and source code to attackers.

Authentication bypass via /webmaster endpoint. The obscurity() function sets session['loggedin'] to a random UUID on line 12 without any authentication check. Any unauthenticated user visiting /webmaster gets a valid session, bypassing the login requirement for protected endpoints like /confidential, /validator, and /monitoring.

Overly permissive CORS configuration. CORS is configured with origins='*' on line 8, allowing any domain to make cross-origin requests to the application. Combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true set in Confidential.py, this enables cross-origin credential theft attacks against authenticated users.

Stored XSS via javascript: URL in messaging link. User-supplied link data is stored in the database and rendered as href="{{item[3]}}" on line 56. While Jinja2 autoescaping prevents HTML injection, it does NOT prevent javascript: protocol URLs. An attacker can submit 'javascript:alert(document.cookie)' as the link value, and any user clicking 'Check Source' will execute the attacker's JavaScript.

Plaintext credentials exposed in HTML template. The webmaster page displays usernames and passwords in an HTML table (lines 23-41): Mark/Thisismysecretpass, Jacob/BirdLover1, Larry/Letmein123. These credentials are accessible to any user who can reach the /webmaster endpoint, which requires no authentication.

Weak password hashing. User passwords are stored as unsalted MD5 hashes (lines 15-18), e.g., '0cef1fb10f60529028a71f58e54ed07b' for Admin. MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsalted hashes are trivially reversible via rainbow tables. Passwords should use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 with unique salts.

Information disclosure via monitoring page. The template executes and displays the output of system commands: 'ps aux | grep 8081' (line 10) and 'cat /etc/passwd' (line 22). This exposes process listing and system user accounts to any user who can access the /monitoring endpoint.

X-XSS-Protection header explicitly disabled. The Search controller sets X-XSS-Protection to '0' on line 11, disabling the browser's built-in XSS filter. This actively removes a defense layer, making the reflected XSS vulnerability on this endpoint easier to exploit.

Access-Control-Allow-Credentials set to true on the confidential data endpoint (line 10). Combined with the wildcard CORS origin in runserver.py, this allows malicious third-party sites to make authenticated cross-origin requests and steal the confidential salary data returned by this endpoint.

False positive: parameterized SQL query. The storeMessages function uses parameterized placeholders (?) on line 13: db.execute('INSERT INTO messages(name, message, link, timestamp)VALUES(?,?,?,?)', (name, message, link, ...)). Despite accepting user-controlled name, message, and link parameters, the use of parameterized queries prevents SQL injection.

False positive: SQL query with no user input. The getMessages function on line 8 executes a static query 'SELECT messageId, name, message, link, timestamp FROM messages' with no user-controlled parameters. Although it constructs a SQL string, no external input can influence the query.

False positive: autoescaped template output. The messaging template renders user-supplied name ({{item[1]}} on line 47) and message ({{item[2]}} on line 52) from the database. Although this is user-controlled data, Jinja2's default autoescaping is enabled (no autoescape false block), so HTML special characters are escaped, preventing XSS through these output points.

False positive: pickle.load on locally-generated data. The /about GET handler (start function) serializes a hardcoded dictionary on line 12-13 and immediately deserializes it on lines 14-15. The pickle file is generated server-side from trusted data — no user input influences the serialized content at this code path. The vulnerable deserialization is in the separate /about/sync POST handler.
